pattern: Walking a grid path is really just tracking a running coordinate and remembering every square you've stood on -- a crossing is the first time the running coordinate repeats a remembered square.
hints: Track your current (x, y) position starting at the origin, and remember every position you've stood on, including the origin itself.
Translate each character into a one-unit shift on one axis: N/S change the vertical coordinate, E/W change the horizontal one.
After each single move, check whether the new position was already remembered before adding it -- the moment it was, the path has crossed itself.

edge_inputs.description: immediately reverses back onto the origin
crossing happens away from the origin, not at start or end
long straight walk in one direction never crosses
a closed square loop crosses exactly when it returns to origin
crossing occurs on the very last move

stress.description: long random walk over all four directions, large enough to likely self-cross while staying well within the length bound
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(1, min(size, 10000))
    path = ''.join(rng.choice('NSEW') for _ in range(n))
    return [path]
